title: SEGMENT 11 — Throughput economics: fewer reads, more bases per read, when it matters

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Throughput economics: fewer reads, more bases per read, when it matters

Let me spend a segment on the economics of PacBio versus its competitors, because the cost structure of long-read sequencing is unintuitive and matters a lot for figuring out when to use which platform.

The basic cost equation in sequencing is: you have a fixed cost per run (the consumables — chip, reagents — plus a fraction of the instrument's amortized cost), and you divide that by the number of bases you generate per run, to get a cost per gigabase.
For Illumina NovaSeq, the cost per gigabase is, roughly, a few dollars at scale.
For PacBio Revio HiFi, it's somewhere in the tens of dollars per gigabase — three to ten times more expensive per base than Illumina.
For Oxford Nanopore on their high-end PromethION, it's in between, somewhere around five to fifteen dollars per gigabase depending on chemistry.
So the headline comparison is: PacBio HiFi data is more expensive per base than Illumina data.
Significantly so.
That's the eternal trade-off — paying for the long reads and the high accuracy.
But the relevant question is not "how much per base" — it's "how much for the answer I'm trying to get.
" And there, the calculus changes depending on the application.
If you want to do a million-sample GWAS — genome-wide association study — where you genotype a few hundred thousand known variants, Illumina is irreplaceable.
PacBio is overkill, and you can't afford it at that scale.
If you want to count RNA transcripts in ten thousand single cells, Illumina is the answer.
PacBio doesn't have the right chemistry or throughput for that workload, although PacBio Iso-Seq has a niche for full-length transcript characterization in smaller numbers of samples.
If you want to do a clinical exome on a patient, Illumina is the answer.
It's cheap, fast, accurate enough, and well-supported by clinical pipelines.

But — and this is the "when it matters" part — if you want to assemble a new genome from scratch, to a reference-quality standard, including the previously unassemblable regions — PacBio HiFi is the answer, not Illumina.

The cost per gigabase is higher, but the cost per assembled genome is lower, because you need much less coverage (thirty-x HiFi is enough for a high-quality human assembly, where you'd need much more Illumina to even attempt the same job, and you still couldn't assemble the repeats).
The number of reads needed is far fewer, but each read does more work.
If you want to catalog structural variants in a tumor or a rare disease patient, HiFi is the answer, because each HiFi read can directly span the event.
Short reads only see structural variants through indirect signals that are unreliable for any but the largest events.
If you want to resolve the two parental copies of a chromosome — phased, diploid assembly — HiFi reads, being long enough to span most heterozygous variants, make this routine.
Short reads collapse the two copies together, and reconstructing phase from short reads requires statistical inference that is often unreliable.
If you want to detect DNA methylation directly, without bisulfite conversion, HiFi (and Nanopore) can do it.
Illumina cannot, fundamentally, because PCR amplification erases the modification.
We'll spend the next segment on methylation.
If you want to characterize a microbial genome to closure, including any plasmids, prophages, and mobile elements — HiFi is the answer.
A single PacBio run on a bacterial isolate gives you a complete, closed, polished assembly.
Illumina can give you a draft, but the draft is fragmented and the repetitive elements are unresolved.
The pattern is: PacBio HiFi wins when accuracy at every position, including in difficult regions, matters more than raw throughput.
The cost per useful answer can be much lower than Illumina, even if the cost per base is higher.

The applications where this is true — de novo assembly, structural variants, phasing, methylation, complete microbial genomics — are exactly the applications where PacBio HiFi has become the standard.

The reverse pattern is: Illumina wins when you're counting things — transcripts, variants at known positions, allele frequencies — and you can tolerate some loss of information in repetitive regions, because the regions you care about are well-behaved.
Most clinical and population-scale work falls in this category, which is why Illumina remains overwhelmingly dominant.
Then there's the niche where PacBio Iso-Seq is unique: full-length transcript sequencing for isoform discovery in a single sample.
We'll cover Iso-Seq specifically in segment fifteen.
And there's the comparison with Nanopore, which we'll dedicate segment sixteen to in detail.
One throughput note: as of the early-2020s, the Revio instrument has dramatically narrowed the cost gap with Illumina for whole-genome sequencing.

A Revio human genome at thirty-x HiFi coverage runs in the low hundreds of dollars — not equal to Illumina, but close enough that for many clinical applications the extra accuracy and SV detection ability is worth it.
PacBio's trajectory is to continue driving this cost down, and they've publicly targeted human genomes in the very-low-hundreds or even sub-hundred range over the next few generations.
If that happens, the cost-per-gigabase argument against PacBio largely disappears, and the platform becomes a real contender for clinical genomics at scale.
We'll come back to this in segment eighteen.
Now let's look at what HiFi can see that other platforms cannot.
Starting with methylation.
